title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

The paper provides a thorough analysis of the label trick, a widely adopted technique in GNNs. The authors derive a deterministic equivalent of the stochastic label trick and show that it can be viewed as a regularization factor that adapts to graph size and connectivity. This is a significant contribution, as it provides a theoretical understanding of the label trick's properties and effects. However, I share the concern that the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and the empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

While the effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work, this paper tries to showcase broader application scenarios. Unfortunately, the results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining Sections 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. Alternative Review 4 also noted that some of the experimental results seem marginal, and it would be helpful to see more significant improvements.

The authors remark that "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." However, as Alternative Review 1 pointed out, this does not necessarily mean that the label trick leads to better performance. To argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training, etc.)? The authors could provide more details on the practical implications of the label trick and its potential limitations, as suggested by Alternative Review 3.

The paper could also benefit from a clearer explanation of the relationship between the label trick and existing methods, such as APPNP and Correct and Smooth, as noted by Alternative Review 2. Additionally, the authors could provide more details on the computational complexity of the proposed methods. Despite these limitations, the paper provides a comprehensive analysis of the label trick and its applications, including theoretical results and experimental evaluations.

In conclusion, while the paper provides a thorough analysis of the label trick, its novel use-cases and empirical analysis are not convincing. The results show that the label trick has little or no benefit in many scenarios, and more significant improvements are needed to demonstrate its effectiveness. Therefore, I maintain a rating of 6, marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
The work presented in the paper is solid, with a rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases, providing a thorough analysis that is both inspiring and insightful. The derivation is presented in a smooth and coherent manner, making it accessible to readers.

The paper's theoretical contributions are significant, as it proves that the stochastic label trick can be reduced to a deterministic training objective composed of two terms: a data-fitting term and a regularization factor conditioned on graph structure. This insight is crucial, as it shows that the label trick can be viewed as a regularization factor that adapts to graph size and connectivity. The authors' analysis is comprehensive, and their results demonstrate the effectiveness of the label trick in various applications, including trainable label propagation, deterministic application to GNNs with linear propagation layers, and trainable Correct and Smooth.

The experiments conducted by the authors verify the efficacy of these extensions and show that the label trick can significantly improve performance on various benchmark datasets. However, to further strengthen the paper, it would be beneficial to have a clearer explanation of the relationship between the label trick and existing methods, such as APPNP and Correct and Smooth. Additionally, providing more details on the computational complexity of the proposed methods would be helpful in understanding their practical implications.

Moreover, while the paper's presentation is generally clear, some sections could benefit from additional clarification. For instance, a clearer introduction to the label trick method and its motivations would help set the stage for the rest of the paper. Furthermore, some of the experimental results, while promising, seem marginal, and it would be helpful to see more significant improvements to fully demonstrate the label trick's potential.

Despite these suggestions for improvement, the paper remains a valuable contribution to the field. The novel use cases introduced by the authors are interesting and promising, and the empirical evaluations justify their potential. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. The paper's strengths outweigh its weaknesses, and its contributions are significant enough to warrant publication.

Rating: 8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

### Summary of the Paper

The paper explores the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (GNNs) and collective classification algorithms, such as label propagation, for node prediction. The authors discuss the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. They provide a theoretical analysis of the label trick, showing that it can be reduced to a deterministic training objective composed of a data-fitting term and a regularization factor conditioned on graph structure.

### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental and important, as noted by the community's interest in research that connects classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks. This intersection is crucial for advancing node prediction techniques.

(+) The approach is well-motivated, particularly in addressing the issue of the identity mapping solution when using the label trick. The authors' intuition that keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix can provide a simple yet effective fix is insightful, as seen in Equation (10).

(+) The work provides a thorough analysis of the label trick, a widely adopted technique in GNNs, and offers novel use cases, including trainable label propagation and deterministic applications to GNNs with linear propagation layers. The experiments verify the efficacy of these extensions and show that the label trick can significantly improve performance.

(–) However, the paper could benefit from further emphasis on related works, such as https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf, which first proposed the label trick. A clearer explanation of the relationship between the label trick and existing methods, like APPNP and Correct and Smooth, would also enhance the paper's context.

(–) Some equations, such as Equation (3), require more intuition. Specifically, expressing the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$ seems unclear. Similarly, the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth needs more explanation.

(–) Given the paper's theoretical flavor, comparisons to other works that compare label and feature propagation, such as Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf), would strengthen the analysis.

(–) The paper's notation is dense, particularly with excessive use of subscripts, making it notionally challenging. A re-write to improve clarity and flow would benefit the reader. For instance, elaboration on Equation 5, specifically the step from the second-last term to the last term, would be appreciated.

(–) In terms of evaluation, for Table 2, using the R^2 value to evaluate regression would be more appropriate.

(–) Section 4.1 requires greater detail, including how the procedure is trained and what the final model entails. References to other papers that learn or modify the adjacency/propagation matrix would also be beneficial.

### Questions and Clarifications

- The deterministic label trick is not used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1; clarification on this and the specific GNN used would be helpful.
- In Remark 4, the assertion that shutting off regularization eliminates the chance for overfitting needs further justification.
- If the derivation shows that the label trick can be decomposed with a regularization term in the deterministic case, its application in Table 3 results should be clarified.

### Minor Points

- A typo in Remark 2 ("insure" → "ensure") and inconsistency in using parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5) should be addressed.

### Conclusion and Rating

The paper tackles an important problem and provides a thorough analysis of the label trick, offering novel use cases and experimental verification. However, it requires further work on the points mentioned above to enhance clarity, context, and comparison with related works.

Given the strengths and weaknesses outlined, I maintain a rating of 5, indicating that the paper is marginally below the acceptance threshold. With revisions addressing the critiques, the paper has the potential to make a significant contribution to the field.

Rating: 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

The paper proposes a label trick method for graph neural networks (GNNs) that enables the parallel use of node features and labels. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the label trick, including its theoretical foundations and applications. 

One of the primary strengths of this paper is its attempt to unify the understanding of the label trick. The authors provide a clear and concise explanation of the method and its advantages, including its ability to be used in conjunction with other methods. For instance, they show that the label trick can be used to improve the performance of GNNs by allowing for the parallel use of node features and labels.

However, there are several areas where the paper could be improved. One major concern is the lack of clarity in the presentation of the paper. For example, the authors could provide more details on the computational complexity of the proposed methods. Additionally, some of the notation used in the paper could be clarified. For instance, the authors use the term "stochastic label trick," but it is not entirely clear what this refers to.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The paper proposes a deterministic version of the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings and provide experiments to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, and the effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

One of the strong points of the paper is the proposal of a relatively new framework for GNNs, where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative that focuses on exclusion of self-propagation. This alternative seems to be novel, and the authors provide a thorough analysis of the label trick, a widely adopted technique in GNNs. They derive a deterministic equivalent of the stochastic label trick and show that it can be viewed as a regularization factor that adapts to graph size and connectivity.

However, I still feel unconvinced about the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except for undersampling. The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated, but what is the deterministic advantage here in terms of accuracy, speed, or any other advantage? The alternative reviews highlight the effectiveness of the label trick in improving performance on various benchmark datasets, but it would be helpful to see a clearer explanation of the relationship between the label trick and existing methods, such as APPNP and Correct and Smooth.

The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1, there is no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g., trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so? One of the alternative reviews suggests that some of the experimental results seem marginal, and it would be helpful to see more significant improvements.

My main concern is that the experimental validation, even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick, is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples, the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick, do not show significant improvement (if at all) when the STD is taken into account. The authors could provide more details on the computational complexity of the proposed methods and a clearer explanation of the practical implications of the label trick and its potential limitations.

The paper provides a valuable result in Theorem 2, which shows an upper bound on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross-entropy loss. However, there are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graphs and the incorporation of label data, such as Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes by Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, and Ronald R. Coifman.

In conclusion, while the paper provides a thorough analysis of the label trick and its applications, I have some fundamental concerns about the experimental validation and motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick. Therefore, I recommend a rating of 5, marginally below the acceptance threshold.

The paper could benefit from a clearer introduction to the label trick method and its motivations, as well as more significant experimental results. Additionally, the authors could provide more details on the computational complexity of the proposed methods and a clearer explanation of the relationship between the label trick and existing methods. With these revisions, the paper could provide a more convincing and comprehensive analysis of the label trick and its applications.

The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:

2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The paper presents a thorough analysis of imitation learning by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. The authors provide a theoretical analysis that certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance between the expert and the imitation learner.

The main contribution of this paper is the reduction of imitation learning to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward for deterministic experts. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the implications of this reduction, including a detailed examination of the theoretical guarantees and the empirical evaluation of the proposed method. Specifically, the authors show that imitation learning can be done by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward, which provides a novel perspective on the problem.

One of the key strengths of this paper is its theoretical analysis, which provides a high-probability performance guarantee for a finite dataset of expert data. The authors also provide a link to adversarial imitation learning algorithms, which is an interesting direction for future research. For instance, the authors could explore the application of their method to more complex tasks, such as those involving multiple agents or partially observable environments.

However, there are also some limitations to the proposed approach. For example, the assumption of a deterministic expert policy may be too restrictive in some cases, and it would be interesting to see how the method performs with stochastic experts. Additionally, the empirical evaluation, while thorough, could be more extensive, and it would be beneficial to see more experiments conducted on a wider range of tasks. Some potential avenues for future work include exploring the use of more sophisticated exploration strategies, such as entropy regularization or curiosity-driven methods.

The authors' use of a stationary reward function is also a simplification that may not always hold in practice. It would be interesting to see how the method performs with non-stationary reward functions or in environments where the reward function is not well-defined. Furthermore, the authors could provide more discussion on the practical implications of their method, including potential applications and limitations.

In terms of related work, the authors provide a good overview of the current state of the field, but could also discuss the relationship between their method and other approaches, such as inverse reinforcement learning or generative adversarial imitation learning. For example, the authors could explore the connection between their method and the maximum likelihood estimation approach used in inverse reinforcement learning.

Overall, the paper provides a well-rounded and thorough analysis of imitation learning by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. While there are some limitations to the proposed approach, the authors provide a solid foundation for future work in this area. With some additional exploration of the assumptions and limitations of the method, this paper could be even stronger.

Rating: 6 - The paper provides a solid contribution to the field of imitation learning, with a thorough analysis of the theoretical guarantees and empirical evaluation of the proposed method. However, there are some limitations to the approach, and additional work is needed to fully explore the implications of the reduction to reinforcement learning. 

The main contributions of this paper are:

*   A thorough analysis of the theoretical implications of imitation learning by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward.
*   A detailed empirical evaluation of the proposed method, including a comparison to other approaches.
*   A discussion of the limitations of the proposed approach, including the assumption of a deterministic expert policy.

The paper is well-written and provides a clear and concise overview of the proposed method and its evaluation. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the theoretical guarantees and empirical results, and the paper is well-organized and easy to follow.

One potential area for future work is the exploration of more sophisticated exploration strategies, such as entropy regularization or curiosity-driven methods. Additionally, the authors could explore the application of their method to more complex tasks, such as those involving multiple agents or partially observable environments.

Overall, the paper provides a solid contribution to the field of imitation learning, and the authors demonstrate a good understanding of the subject matter. The paper is well-written and provides a clear and concise overview of the proposed method and its evaluation.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

reviews.text:
The paper presents a method for imitation learning by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. The authors provide a thorough theoretical analysis that certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance between the expert and the imitation learner. The authors also conduct experiments that confirm their reduction works well in practice for continuous control tasks.

One of the key contributions of this paper is the reduction of imitation learning to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. This reduction is significant because it allows for the application of well-established reinforcement learning techniques to imitation learning problems. The authors provide a thorough analysis of the theoretical implications of this reduction, including a high-probability performance guarantee for a finite dataset of expert data.

However, as noted in one of the alternative reviews, the paper could benefit from a clearer explanation of the assumptions made, particularly with regards to the deterministic expert policy. The authors assume that the expert policy is deterministic, which may not always be the case. This assumption has implications for the applicability of the method, and the authors should provide more discussion on the limitations of their approach.

The empirical evaluation of the method is thorough, with experiments conducted on continuous control tasks. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, with competitive performance compared to other imitation learning algorithms. However, as noted in one of the alternative reviews, the experiments could be more extensive, and the authors could provide more details on the implementation and hyperparameters used.

One potential area for improvement is the explanation of the practical implications of the method. While the authors provide a thorough theoretical analysis, they could do more to explain how their method could be applied in practice. For example, they could provide more details on how the method could be used in real-world applications, or how it could be integrated with existing reinforcement learning systems.

In terms of organization, the paper could benefit from clearer transitions between sections. For example, the authors could provide a clearer introduction to the problem of imitation learning, and then explain how their method addresses this problem. Additionally, the authors could provide more detailed explanations of the theoretical results, including proofs and derivations.

Overall, the paper presents a significant contribution to the field of imitation learning, with a thorough theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation. However, there are areas for improvement, particularly with regards to the explanation of assumptions and practical implications. With some revisions to address these areas, the paper has the potential to be a strong addition to the literature.

Rating: 6 - The paper presents a solid contribution to the field of imitation learning, with a thorough theoretical analysis and empirical evaluation. However, there are areas for improvement, particularly with regards to the explanation of assumptions and practical implications. 

The main changes I suggest are 

1. Provide more explanation of the assumptions made, particularly with regards to the deterministic expert policy.
2. Provide more details on the implementation and hyperparameters used in the experiments.
3. Clarify the practical implications of the method, including how it could be applied in real-world applications.
4. Consider adding more related work to provide a better context for the proposed method.

These changes will help to strengthen the paper and make it more suitable for publication.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.
reviews.main_review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.

Main Review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper presents a method for imitation learning by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. The authors provide a thorough theoretical analysis that certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance between the expert and the imitation learner. The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods.

One of the key contributions of this paper is the provision of a high-probability performance guarantee for a finite dataset of expert data, which links the reduction to adversarial imitation learning algorithms. However, I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic, as noted in Alternative Review 1. The authors should provide more explanation on this, particularly in terms of how it affects the practical implications of the method.

The theoretical guarantees provided are sound and well-established, as pointed out in Alternative Review 2. The authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. However, this assumption of a deterministic expert policy may not always be the case, and the authors should provide more discussion on the limitations of their approach and potential avenues for future work, as suggested in Alternative Review 4.

The empirical evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed method, with competitive performance with other imitation learning algorithms on continuous control tasks. However, as noted in Alternative Review 2, some recent baselines are missing, and the authors could provide a more detailed comparison to existing approaches. Additionally, the experiments could be more extensive, and the authors could provide more details about the implementation and hyperparameters used, as suggested in Alternative Review 3.

In terms of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problems with bad convergence rates. It would be clearer if the authors could provide a comparison of computation times of different algorithms in the experiment section, as mentioned in the main review. This would help to better understand the practical implications of the method.

Overall, this paper provides a rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning, with a novel approach that shows potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct, and the result gives intuition on how this method performs in terms of expected return. While there are some limitations and potential areas for improvement, the paper is well-written, clear, and well-organized.

Rating: 6 - marginally above the acceptance threshold.

Recommendation: While the paper shows promise and provides a thorough theoretical analysis, it requires more discussion on the limitations of the approach, particularly with regards to the assumption of a deterministic expert policy and the sparse reward function. Additionally, more extensive experiments and a detailed comparison to existing approaches would strengthen the paper. Therefore, I marginally recommend acceptance, with revisions addressing these concerns.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers imitation learning problem which aims at obtaining a policy that imitate expert behavior. Authors considers using reinforcement learning with a stationary reward constructed by expert datasets. Through theoretical analysis, the corresponding imitation policy is proved to achieve high expected per-step intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward. The difference between the expert policy and the imitation policy is bounded with a high probability. Some empirical experiments are performed on continuous control tasks. The results show this method is comparable with other algorithms while the algorithm is simpler.

Main Review:
The idea of using reinforcement learning to imitate expert behavior is simple to execute compared to other imitation learning methods. I have a concern regarding the sparse reward function used in this paper. When training a reinforcement learning model, the sparse reward becomes problematic when you apply RL algorithm to solve it. Although the better intrinsic reward leads to better extrinsic reward, it is still troublesome to get a good policy by any reinforcement learning algorithm with this sparse reward function. This makes the result less useful than just applying other comparison methods. Could you give more explanation on this?
In term of computation time, the sparse reward can cause problem of bad convergence rate. It is much clearer if you can provide comparison of computation time of different algorithms in the experiment section. 
Overall, this paper is well written and very clear to readers. The result is promising and interesting from a theoretical point of view.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper provides rigorous analysis for using reinforcement learning in imitation learning. The result is novel and shows the potential to accomplish imitation learning in a straightforward way. The intrinsic reward function reduced by the expert dataset is simple to construct. By setting a constant one reward for matching the demonstrated action in a demonstrated state, any reinforcement learning algorithm can provide policy for such a MDP problem. The result in this paper gives intuition of how this method performs in terms of expected return. And sufficient experimental results are shown to verify their theoretical results.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose an algorithm for imitation learning which rewards the agent for observing state-action pairs that are part of the demonstration-set. The main contribution of the paper is the theoretical analysis which shows that the algorithm, given sufficient expert data, matches the expert’s occupancy distribution and expected reward. The proof comes in three parts: first, the authors relate expert rollouts to the limiting distribution of the expert’s policy. Second, the authors put a probabilistic bound on the expected agreement with expert-data based on the mixing time of the agent. Finally, the authors use this bound on expected agreement to bound the difference in expected extrinsic reward. The authors also provide an empirical evaluation in standard control benchmarks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper is generally well written and easy to follow; however, the structure is sometimes unusual. The proofs would be easier to follow if the authors moved Lemmas from Appendix C & D into the main body while moving some of the detailed proofs from the main body into the Appendix.

The derived bound is non-trivial, but it is unclear how significant it is. The main issue lies in the assumption on the amount of data that is required which is linear (with a factor > 1) in the squared size of the state-space (note that the bound remains large even if \delta is set to 1). Such a large amount of expert data is required since rewarding agreement is not a valid strategy when expert data is sparse: imagine a sub-sampled expert trajectory where the agent has the capability to return to previously seen states. In a more typical distribution-matching objective, the agent has to visit all the demonstration states and thus still has to complete the trajectory. In the objective proposed by the authors, the agent can maximize reward by returning to the same demonstration-state repeatedly. Naturally, if demonstrations are available in all states and the agent’s representational capacity is unlimited, then maximizing agreement is trivially optimal. The author’s bound does not require demonstrations to be available in all states and may thus be useful; however, the required amount of expert data is extremely large and no statement can be made if less data is available.

In the related works section, the authors claim that the method is minimizing the GAIL-objective. This is misleading; the GAIL objective is equivalent to minimizing the JS divergence to the expert histogram for all choices of N while the proposed method only minimizes TV distance and thus JS divergence if N is large enough. 

For the empirical evaluation, the authors use an L2-distance as a stand-in for the reward used in the analysis. This is necessary as the reward is undefined in continuous state-action spaces (similarly, it will be too sparse in large discrete state-action spaces), but the implications of that heuristic are not explored in the paper. It would be good if the authors could add motivation and discussion for this form of the reward. The results are very positive, but it is unclear whether they are due to the theoretical properties of the algorithm or due to the particular nature of the mujoco walkers. Since the reward is a part of the observation-space in these domains, using simple heuristics like squared distance can plausibly lead to high performance; however, this would not generalize to other domains. It is furthermore problematic that the hardest benchmark task, humanoid, is missing as the given baseline algorithms have no problem solving it. Finally, the reported numbers for behavioral cloning are significantly worse than what has previously been reported in the literature.
